bull_thesis: Logista is a cash-rich, high-return distribution business trading at roughly a 6% dividend yield (about EUR 2.09 a share on a EUR 33 price), with a balance sheet that is close to net cash. The market is treating it as a shrinking tobacco annuity, when in reality it is a slowly compounding, diversified cash machine. The quality is real and the financials back it up: return on equity of 41.3%, return on invested capital of 19.6%, an Altman-Z score of 3.1 (a bankruptcy-risk gauge, where above 3 signals safety), a Piotroski score of 6 out of 9 (a nine-point financial-health checklist), and a corrected net-debt figure of only EUR 184M sitting against a roughly EUR 2bn cash float Logista holds for Imperial Brands. The screener's EUR 733M net-debt figure is a data error that overstates the risk, so the headline solvency scare is false. The top line is genuinely growing, from EUR 12.43B to EUR 12.99B to EUR 13.54B in revenue, up 4.2% year over year, a 5.7% compound annual growth rate over three years. The diversification is working where it counts: non-tobacco now makes up more than 52% of economic sales, pharma is growing 10 to 13%, and the courier business is growing high single digits, with management delivering real cost savings (integrating the El Mosca freight routes, consolidating the Carbo parcel network, and targeting EUR 6 to 8M of operating-profit synergies). The deep-value case (buying a stock for clearly less than its underlying worth) is that the market extrapolates terminal tobacco decline and the El Mosca/Carbo cyclical drag forever, while underweighting three things: a balance sheet that funds the dividend without borrowing; profit-on-inventory (gains booked when tobacco prices rise on stock already held) that has surprised to the upside three years running, from EUR 25M to EUR 35M to EUR 45M as governments keep raising tobacco taxes; and a recent positive turn, with latest-quarter earnings per share up 84% year over year and trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings per share of 3.02. The disagreement with the market is one of asymmetry: a discounted-cash-flow valuation puts fair value at 64.3 (a 49% margin of safety), the Graham method is up 53%, the owner-earnings method up 56%, and the cash-flow method up 49% -- four independent models all say the stock is worth materially more, treating today's cash flows as durable. The dividend is a cornerstone the Board has explicitly committed to defend, with a 12-year, roughly EUR 2bn-since-listing track record and a 236% total return. A few conditions must hold for the case to work: European Central Bank rates do not collapse far below about 2% (which would preserve meaningful income on the unhedged roughly EUR 1bn of float, on top of the roughly 50% hedged at about 3.6% through June 2027); tobacco-tax-driven profit-on-inventory keeps recurring at EUR 30M or more; and the El Mosca/Carbo turnaround stabilizes Iberian transport margins through 2026, with the promised one-off restructuring charges genuinely confined to fiscal 2025. If those hold, an investor collects a covered roughly 6% yield while the cyclical parts recover and the valuation re-rates toward the cash-flow view.
bear_thesis: Logista is a slowly shrinking tobacco annuity whose surface cheapness is propped up by two fading, low-quality tailwinds that management itself disowns, with no firm catalyst to force the price to converge with fair value -- a classic value-and-yield trap. The record is clear: earnings peaked in fiscal 2024 (net income of EUR 308M, earnings per share of 2.34) and rolled over in fiscal 2025 (net income of EUR 281M, down 9%, earnings per share of 2.13, down 9%), and consensus forward earnings growth is just 0.5% -- the market already expects a stall. The two engines that flattered 2023 to 2024 are structurally impaired. Financial income on the cash float has fallen from EUR 103M to EUR 73M as the European Central Bank's average rate dropped from 4.85% to 3.54%, and it keeps melting as rates normalize (management hedged only about 50% and guided net financial income down to about EUR 65 to 70M in real time). And profit-on-inventory (gains booked when tobacco prices rise on stock already held; EUR 45M in fiscal 2025, about 16% of net income) is entirely outside management's hands -- they repeat that it is 'not under our control... the manufacturers deciding when and how they increase prices' and refuse to forecast it. Strip out profit-on-inventory and a normalized float, and the earnings-power view is damning: on earnings power value (the worth of current, sustainable profits with no growth credit) the margin of safety is -100%, and a Buffett-style fair value of 22.8 sits 25% below the EUR 33 price -- on normalized earnings power, for a distributor with a 2.1% net margin and a 3.6% and contracting gross margin, the stock is expensive, not cheap. The diversification that was sold as reducing risk has instead imported cyclicality: El Mosca (long-haul) and Carbo (frozen) are in multi-quarter restructuring with 'sluggish demand,' 'margin compression,' and outright 'loss of clients against competitors,' and management has had to throttle its acquisition growth engine ('more cautious... the fair answer is yes') and shelve the 'transformational' deal it floated in fiscal 2024. The loudest warning is the dividend: to hold the payout flat, the payout ratio was pushed to 99% 'despite the need to increase the payout ratio' -- the coverage cushion is gone, and one more year of fading profit-on-inventory or float income, or a deeper transport drag, forces a real cut. Cash quality confirms the fragility: trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is only EUR 66M against roughly EUR 280M of net income, a collapse in conversion that contradicts the 'cheap at 7.3x price-to-free-cash-flow' screen. Other evidence to watch: an insider buy ratio of 0.00 and a smart-money score of 26 out of 100 (no insider or institutional conviction); a legacy tobacco core in irreversible secular decline (traditional Spain down 0.8%, France 'almost impossible to reverse'); any fiscal 2026 El Mosca/Carbo charges that break the 'will not roll over to 26' promise; and the European Central Bank cutting further. With the stock at 81% of its 52-week range and 8% above its 200-day moving average, the market is not pricing terminal decline -- it is pricing the peak tailwinds as durable. There is no buyback authorization, no activist, no asset sale, no spin-off, nothing to force a re-rating -- only a fully paid-out dividend and a self-help turnaround at the mercy of the European freight cycle and tobacco-tax politics.
consensus_delta: Looked at as a deep-value name (one bought for clearly less than its underlying worth), the question is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into stable or growing earnings power -- and the honest answer is no; the opposite risk is the live one. With the stock at 81% of its 52-week range, 8% above its 200-day moving average, and near 7.3x headline price-to-free-cash-flow, the market is effectively treating peak, fading tailwinds as if they were durable, which is the reverse of a deep-value mispricing. What is genuinely mispriced is the net-debt data error (a live EUR 184M versus the EUR 733M scan figure) and a near-net-cash balance sheet behind a roughly 6% yield -- that is real and underappreciated. But the assumptions the bull case leans on are the shaky ones: first, that profit-on-inventory (gains booked when tobacco prices rise on stock already held; EUR 45M, about 16% of net income) is a recurring earnings line, when management explicitly disowns control and refuses to guide it; second, that float financial income holds, when it has already fallen from EUR 103M to EUR 73M with the European Central Bank's easing cycle and management itself guided it down to about EUR 65 to 70M; and third, that the discounted-cash-flow fair value of 64.3 is reachable, when it treats today's elevated cash flows as durable, while the earnings-power model (margin of safety -100%) and the Buffett fair value of 22.8 (below the price) capture the normalized reality of a 2.1%-net-margin distributor. The disagreement that actually matters is not 'the market is too bearish' -- it is that both the bull models (cash flow up 49%) and the bear models (earnings power -100%) are 'right' depending on whether you believe the rate-float and profit-on-inventory windfalls repeat. Under the current regime read (higher-for-longer in the US, but the European Central Bank is the relevant central bank here and is actively cutting, with its reference rate moving from 4.5% to 2.65% over the comparison period), the float-income engine keeps eroding, which settles the disagreement in the bear's favor. This is a thesis that hinges on shared macro forces (the European Central Bank's rate path, tobacco-tax politics, and the European freight cycle), with no independent, company-specific driver to resolve it -- exactly the kind of dependence the regime tells us to be wary of.
forcing_function: For a deep-value name, the catalyst that forces a re-rating has to be a concrete event -- a buyback authorization, an activist, an asset sale, a spin-off, or a dividend start or step-up. Logista has none of these in the pipeline. There is no buyback (capital is fully committed to the 99% dividend payout), no activist, no process to value and separate the business lines, no announced asset sale, and, critically, the acquisition growth engine has been throttled rather than switched on ('more cautious... the fair answer is yes'; the fiscal 2024 'transformational' deal 'put on hold'). The only firm dates are scheduled, low-energy, or fade-prone: the February 2026 annual meeting merely ratifies an already-announced flat dividend (a non-event, not a re-rating catalyst), and the next earnings report (about 49 days out) is more likely to confirm the fading of profit-on-inventory and float income than to break a bearish consensus -- and the consensus here is not even bearish (the stock sits at 81% of its 52-week range). Under the regime lens this is decisive: the framework prizes firm-dated catalysts inside a favorable window and tilts the book toward special situations in the deep, thinly covered corners (distressed and deleveraging names ahead of spin-offs ahead of forced sellers), in a hot deal-making and open-regulator environment where such catalysts are plentiful. Logista sits in none of those corners -- it is a healthy, near-net-cash, no-catalyst dividend annuity -- so the cost of holding it instead of the live special situations on offer is high. The absence of any catalyst, in a regime overflowing with dated structural ones elsewhere, is the single strongest reason to pass rather than wait.
valley_of_death: For a deep-value name, the risky window is whatever could break the thesis before the gap to fair value closes -- and here it is not a liquidation or a debt-maturity wall (the balance sheet is sound: live net debt of EUR 184M, an Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 3.1, a roughly EUR 2bn float, and no refinancing cliff), but a slow bleed in earnings quality with no offsetting catalyst over the next 3 to 9 months. Three concrete drags fall inside that window. First, the first-half fiscal 2026 results (about 49 days out, late July 2026) carry high fade risk -- profit-on-inventory (gains booked when tobacco prices rise on stock already held) is lumpy and un-guided and could come in well below the EUR 45M of fiscal 2025, while management has pre-committed that the El Mosca/Carbo 'one-off' restructuring costs 'will not roll over to 26,' a promise that, if broken with fresh charges, would directly damage credibility and the print. Second, financial income keeps melting as the European Central Bank eases (already down from EUR 103M to EUR 73M, guided to about EUR 65 to 70M), a structural drag on net income every quarter regardless of how operations do. Third, the El Mosca long-haul and Carbo frozen turnaround is unfinished and demand-dependent -- 'sluggish demand across Europe,' 'margin compression,' 'loss of clients against competitors' -- and a continued European freight downturn keeps Iberian transport margins compressed (Iberian adjusted operating profit was already down 5% in fiscal 2025). None of these wreck free cash flow catastrophically, but they grind the story lower precisely when there is no positive catalyst to counter them, and the 99% payout means any extra fade pressures the dividend directly. The asymmetric near-term risk is a dividend-coverage scare or a soft first-half print de-rating the stock from its 81%-of-range perch before any value can be realized.
positioning_washout: Logista's shareholders are yield-and-income holders -- this is a roughly 6% dividend payer with a 12-year, roughly EUR 2bn-since-listing distribution record and a Board that treats the dividend as a cornerstone it will defend. That creates a specific, one-sided mechanical risk: the dividend is now funded at a 99% payout ratio with no coverage cushion ('despite the need to increase the payout ratio'). The yield-focused and index-tracking base is stable only as long as the headline dividend holds; if a further fade in profit-on-inventory or financial income, or a deeper transport drag, forces even a modest cut, the income holders sell mechanically, and there is no growth or value buyer positioned to absorb them, because the name screens as a low-growth, low-margin distributor with no catalyst. There is no forced-selling tailwind to buy into here -- no index-deletion overhang to fade, no Russell-reconstitution dynamic, no antitrust-divestiture forced seller; it sits in none of the regime's deep forced-flow corners -- so the mechanical setup is purely a downside risk: a dividend cut would turn the income base into forced sellers. An insider buy ratio of 0.00 and a smart-money score of 26 out of 100 confirm there is no informed accumulation underpinning the stock. In short, the setup is the wrong shape for an aggressive entry -- the only mechanical flow primed to fire is a wave of selling on a coverage scare, not a squeeze or a painful move higher.
moderator_conclusion: The recommendation is to pass for now, with low conviction (a 2 on our scale). Logista is a high-quality, cash-rich, near-net-cash distribution business (return on equity 41.3%, return on invested capital 19.6%, live net debt of EUR 184M, an Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 3.1) paying a real roughly 6% dividend with a 12-year record -- this is not a story of capital destruction, and the screener's EUR 733M net-debt scare is a confirmed data error. But it is the wrong instrument for this committee and this regime. The deep-value label does not hold up: on normalized earnings power the stock is not cheap (earnings-power margin of safety -100%, Buffett fair value of 22.8, below the EUR 33 price); the apparent cheapness (discounted cash flow up 49%, 7.3x headline price-to-free-cash-flow) is propped up by two fading, low-quality tailwinds that management itself disowns -- profit-on-inventory (gains booked when tobacco prices rise on stock already held; exogenous tobacco-tax windfalls, about 16% of net income, 'not under our control') and float financial income (already melting from EUR 103M to EUR 73M as the European Central Bank eases, guided down to about EUR 65 to 70M). Earnings peaked in fiscal 2024 and rolled 9% lower in fiscal 2025; the dividend is now defended at a 99% payout with no coverage cushion; the 'diversification' legs (El Mosca, Carbo) imported European-freight cyclicality and are mid-restructuring; trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is just EUR 66M against roughly EUR 280M of net income; insiders are absent (buy ratio 0.00). Decisively, there is no catalyst to force a re-rating -- no buyback, no activist, no spin-off, no asset sale, acquisitions throttled -- and the thesis hinges on shared macro forces (the European Central Bank's rate path, tobacco-tax politics, the freight cycle) with no independent, company-specific driver. Applying the regime framework moves this firmly to pass: the regime rewards firm-dated catalysts in a favorable window and tilts hard toward special situations in the deep, thinly covered corners (distressed and deleveraging names, spin-offs, forced sellers) in a hot deal-making and open-regulator environment where such catalysts are plentiful -- and Logista sits in none of them, while its one identifiable lever (rate-sensitive float income) is being eroded by the very European Central Bank easing cycle underway, the kind of rate-dependent driver the regime penalizes. This is neither an aggressive entry (no catalyst, the market is not offside, no primed pain trade) nor even a clean watchlist candidate (the next dated event is a fade-risk earnings print, not a flush that sets up a re-rating). It is a coupon-clipper's yield-and-quality name, not an event-driven value position -- and against the live special situations the regime points to, the opportunity cost is too high. The conviction is a 2 rather than a 1 only because the balance sheet and dividend genuinely reduce the risk of permanent capital loss; the upside edge is dead.
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup — Logista (LOG.MC) | Industrials / Distribution | Signal: deep value

*Source material: four consecutive results calls (first-half 2024, full-year 2024, first-half 2025, full-year 2025) cross-referenced against the screener's multi-year financial trajectory and the latest trailing-twelve-month and latest-quarter overrides.*

---

## 1. The story arc

Logista's story across these four calls is the slow, managed swap of one earnings engine for another -- and the quiet discovery that the replacement engine is worse than advertised.

**The opening frame (first-half 2024).** Management leads with "a solid set of results, thanks to the growth of different business lines, **the profit on inventory** recorded for the period and **the evolution of the interest rates**" (first-half 2024). Both load-bearing tailwinds are named in the first breath: profit-on-inventory (gains booked when tobacco prices and taxes rise on stock already held) and financial income on the roughly EUR 2bn Imperial Brands cash float. The structural story is *diversification away from tobacco* -- "maintaining the ratio of non-tobacco related economic sales higher than 50%" -- carried out through bolt-on acquisitions (SGEL Libros, Belgium Parcels Service, El Mosca, Speedlink). The February 2024 credit-line addendum to "partially hedge the potential interest rate risk for the next 3 years" is presented as prudent foresight; in hindsight it is management bracing for the financial-income engine to roll over.

**The middle of the arc (full-year 2024).** Confidence peaks. Net profit of EUR 308M (up 13%), the dividend raised 30% to EUR 2.09 a share, financial income of EUR 93M "thanks to the higher average interest rate." Diversification is celebrated -- 100% ownership consummated across El Mosca and Speedlink, "6 transactions... surpass the 50% threshold." But the first crack is audible: long-haul transport is "tightening," with Red Sea disruption and "excess capacity in the market." Management is still on the front foot, talking up the acquisition pipeline ("similar size to El Mosca, why not").

**The pivot (first-half 2025 into full-year 2025).** The story inverts. The *diversification asset* becomes the *problem child*. By first-half 2025, El Mosca and the Carbo frozen business "has suffered from macroeconomic situation... lower European demand," and management is no longer pitching growth -- it is itemizing a turnaround: "new management," "logistics control measures and compliance procedures," "optimizing the client mix," route integration with Logista Freight. By full-year 2025, the language is fully defensive: El Mosca "has had a challenging year," "sluggish demand across Europe and margin compression," Carbo suffered "increasing competition, which has led to the loss of clients against competitors." At the same time the financial-income engine is in visible decline -- EUR 103M (full-year 2024) to EUR 73M (full-year 2025) as the European Central Bank rate fell from a 4.85% to a 3.54% average. The dividend, once *raised*, is now *defended*: full-year 2025 holds it flat at EUR 277M via a **99% payout ratio**, explicitly "despite the need to increase the payout ratio."

**Growth drivers introduced, then muted.** The "big/transformational acquisition in Europe in 2025" floated at full-year 2024 is walked back by first-half 2025 ("the number big or transformational is not part of our aim") and by full-year 2025 reframed as "put on hold a couple of opportunities" while management restructures El Mosca. The recycle next-generation-products initiative, hyped early, is conceded to be "around 1% to 2% of the EBIT coming from Italy" -- immaterial. In short, the bull drivers (diversification growth, transformational acquisitions, new revenue lines) have all been trimmed, while the two engines that actually move the profit-and-loss statement (profit-on-inventory, financial income) are the two management controls least.

---

## 2. Claims versus financials (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The actual numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Solid set of results... growth of different business lines" | every call | Revenue EUR 12.43B to EUR 12.99B to EUR 13.54B (up 4.2% year over year, 5.7% three-year compound growth); economic sales up 3 to 4%. The top line is genuinely, if slowly, growing. | **Matches** -- modest, real top-line growth |
| Net profit strength / dividend coverage | first-half 2024, full-year 2024 | Net income of EUR 272M in 2023 to EUR 308M in 2024 (up 13%), then **EUR 281M in 2025 (down 9%)**. Earnings per share of 2.07 to 2.34 to **2.13 (down 9% year over year)**. The peak was full-year 2024; full-year 2025 gave back the gain. | **Diverges** -- earnings *peaked and rolled*; the "solid" framing masks a down year |
| Financial income is a durable contributor | first-half 2024 ("evolution of interest rates") | EUR 53M (first-half 2024) to EUR 93M (full-year 2024) to EUR 73M (full-year 2025), and falling as the European Central Bank cuts. About 50% hedged at about 3.6%; the unhedged roughly EUR 1bn float reprices down with Euribor. | **Diverges** -- explicitly a *melting* tailwind; management's own hedge admits it |
| Profit-on-inventory is a recurring earnings lever | full-year 2025 ("third consecutive year... substantial profit on inventory") | EUR 25M (first-half 2024) to EUR 35M (full-year 2024) to **EUR 45M (full-year 2025)**. Real and rising -- *but* management repeatedly warns it is "not under our control... the manufacturers... deciding when and how they increase prices." | **Partially matches** -- real cash, but low quality (driven by outside forces, tobacco-tax-dependent, one-off in character) |
| Diversification de-risks the business | every call | The diversified assets (El Mosca long-haul, Carbo frozen) are precisely what *broke* in 2025, importing the cyclical European-freight risk the legacy tobacco book never had. Return on equity of 41.3% and return on invested capital of 19.6% are still elite -- but driven by asset-light tobacco plus the cash float, not the new cyclical legs. | **Diverges** -- diversification *added* cyclicality and a turnaround liability rather than removing risk |
| Free-cash-flow / cash-generation quality | screener | Scan shows price-to-free-cash-flow of 7.3x and free cash flow up 58.9% year over year -- but the **trailing-twelve-month free-cash-flow override is just EUR 66M** versus roughly EUR 280M of net income. Cash conversion has collapsed (working-capital swings in tobacco distribution plus transport capital spending). | **Diverges sharply** -- the headline "7.3x free cash flow / value" screen is contradicted by EUR 66M trailing free cash flow; the cheap-on-cash-flow thesis is fragile |
| Balance sheet | screener override | The scan's net debt of EUR 733M is **wrong**; a live recompute gives **EUR 184M net debt, effectively near net cash** given the EUR 2bn Imperial float sits against it. Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 3.1 (safe), Piotroski financial-health score of 6 out of 9. | **Green** -- the balance sheet is sound; the headline-debt scare is a data artifact |

**Pulling the forensic read together:** The valuation models violently disagree for a *structural* reason. The Buffett / earnings-power-style fair value (EUR 22.8; earnings-power margin of safety of **-100%**) treats Logista as a low-margin (2.1% net, 3.6% gross) distributor whose *normalized* earnings power -- stripped of profit-on-inventory windfalls and a peak-rate cash float -- is thin and shrinking. The discounted-cash-flow fair value (EUR 64.3, up 49%) treats the *current* elevated cash flows (profit-on-inventory plus financial income at recent highs) as if they were durable. The truth sits in between: at EUR 33 the stock is **not cheap on normalized earnings power** but **is supported by a fat, well-covered (until now) dividend and a near-net-cash balance sheet**. The deep-value label is valid only if you believe profit-on-inventory keeps recurring and rates do not fully normalize away the float income -- both of which management itself flags as outside its control.

---

## 3. Tone and confidence trajectory

**Trajectory: declining (offensive to defensive over four calls).**

- **First-half 2024 -- confident/offensive.** "Solid set of results"; active acquisitions ("we continue to seek new transactions"); growth-line emphasis. Caveats are external (Red Sea, weather). *Specific on the tailwinds, vague on the float roll-off.*
- **Full-year 2024 -- peak confidence.** Dividend up 30%; "transformational... why not"; "relatively more positive for '25." This is the high-water mark of the story.
- **First-half 2025 -- turning defensive.** Opens by flagging El Mosca/Carbo weakness; introduces turnaround vocabulary ("new management," "compliance procedures," "optimize the client mix"). Net profit down 5%, blamed on "lower interest rates." Still claims "EBIT in line with market expectations" but hedges heavily ("too soon," "not under our control").
- **Full-year 2025 -- fully defensive.** "Challenging year," "sluggish demand," "margin compression," "loss of clients against competitors." Dividend now *defended* not grown, via a 99% payout. Acquisitions reframed from offense to "put on hold... focused on integration and restructuring." Explicit admission of caution: on whether El Mosca makes them more cautious on acquisitions, "The fair answer is to say, yes."

The arc is a clean, one-way slide from growth-and-investment language to efficiency-and-cost-and-restructuring language -- the classic late-cycle distributor signature.

---

## 4. Guidance credibility

Logista guides loosely and on purpose -- "adjusted EBIT mid-single-digit growth **excluding** profit on inventory" is the only firm frame, and it is deliberately profit-on-inventory-agnostic because that line "is not under our control."

- **On the ex-profit-on-inventory EBIT guide:** *delivered.* First-half 2024 was "fully aligned with our guidance of mid-single digit excluding inventory revaluation reaching 5%." Reliable on the metric they can steer.
- **On profit-on-inventory itself:** they refuse to forecast it ("It's quite difficult to foresee the exact number," first-half 2024) and proved right to -- it has been volatile (EUR 25M / EUR 35M / EUR 45M). Honest about the uncertainty, but it means the *most material* swing factor is un-guided.
- **On the dividend:** they promised "at least the same dividend as 2024" and delivered it -- but only by pushing the payout to **99%**, which the full-year 2024 framing ("at least 90% of net profit") did not telegraph. The commitment held; the *quality* of how it held (no longer covered with a buffer) is a downgrade.
- **On the "transformational acquisition" floated at full-year 2024:** *missed / quietly retracted.* By full-year 2025 it is "on hold." Analyst Jorge Orte called this out directly: "In November, Logista said it was considering a big acquisition... Why is that being delayed?" Management's walk-back ("more than a delay... we put on hold") is a soft miss.

**Earnings-delivery context:** earnings per share grew at a 12.2% three-year compound rate, but the *latest* prints are deteriorating -- full-year earnings per share down 9%, consensus forward earnings growth of 0.5% (the market already models a flat-to-stalling year). Note the override: **latest-quarter earnings per share up 84% year over year and trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings per share of 3.02** -- a sharp positive quarterly turn that sits *against* the down-9% fiscal-year read, likely driven by profit-on-inventory and seasonality and not yet proven durable.

**Reliability: medium.** They hit what they steer (ex-profit-on-inventory EBIT, the dividend), are candid about what they cannot (profit-on-inventory), but over-promised on transformational acquisitions and let dividend coverage quietly erode to defend the headline payout.

---

## 5. Analyst pressure points

Recurring clusters of pressure, and where they led:

1. **"Will the dividend survive falling profit?"** (Kepler, full-year 2024: "in the case that net profit drops in 2025 due to lower profit-on-inventory and financial income, do you intend to increase your payout ratio?"). Management initially gave reassurance about "the Board... completely aligned"; the bad-news payoff arrived at full-year 2025 as the **99% payout** -- exactly the forced payout-ratio expansion analysts feared.
2. **El Mosca / long-haul deterioration.** Pressed every call from first-half 2024 onward. Management's answers degraded from "too premature" (first-half 2024) to a full restructuring confession (full-year 2025). Analysts smelled this early; management was slow to concede the scale.
3. **Profit-on-inventory normalization** ("reasonable level of profit-on-inventory for the year," BNP, repeatedly). Persistent deflection ("difficult to foresee," "not under our control") -- legitimate, but it leaves the single biggest earnings driver permanently un-modelable, a real risk the market underweights.
4. **The financial-income cliff.** Analysts repeatedly probed the net-financial-income run-rate at current Euribor (Francisco Ruiz, first-half 2025: "best estimate for net financial income... at current Euribor?"). Management gave about EUR 65 to 70M -- in other words, *guided the tailwind down* in real time. The market that bid the stock to 81% of its 52-week range may be slow to price this melt.
5. **"Transformational acquisition -- where is it?"** Pushed by multiple desks; answered with retraction. The growth optionality analysts were underwriting has been removed.

The through-line: analysts have been *correctly* worried about (a) dividend coverage, (b) the cyclical transport drag, and (c) the twin fade of profit-on-inventory plus float income. Management has been candid but late on each.

---

## 6. Red flags / green flags

**Red flags**
- Earnings rolled over (full-year 2025 net income down 9%, earnings per share down 9%) after peaking in full-year 2024 -- the diversification story did not prevent a down year; it caused part of it.
- A 99% dividend payout to hold the headline payout flat -- the coverage buffer is gone; any further fade in profit-on-inventory or financial income forces a real cut.
- Two of the three "diversification" tailwinds are driven by outside forces and fading -- financial income (EUR 103M to EUR 73M, falling with European Central Bank cuts) and profit-on-inventory (tobacco-tax-dependent, "not under our control").
- The diversification assets are the cyclical liability -- El Mosca plus Carbo importing European-freight cyclicality, client losses to competitors, multi-quarter restructuring with un-quantified "one-off" costs ("only applicable to fiscal year 2025").
- Trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of EUR 66M versus roughly EUR 280M of net income -- cash conversion has collapsed; the "cheap on free cash flow" screen (7.3x) is contradicted by the override.
- Insider buy ratio of 0.00, smart-money score of 26 out of 100 -- no insider or institutional conviction signal.
- The tobacco core is in structural decline (traditional Spain down 0.8%, France "almost impossible to reverse") -- the cash-generative legacy engine shrinks each year.

**Green flags**
- A near-net-cash balance sheet (live net debt of EUR 184M against the EUR 2bn float; Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 3.1, Piotroski financial-health score of 6 out of 9) -- no solvency or refinancing risk; the EUR 733M scan figure is a data error.
- Elite returns on the core -- return on equity of 41.3%, return on invested capital of 19.6%; the asset-light tobacco-distribution franchise plus the float remains a cash machine.
- Genuine, if slow, top-line diversification -- non-tobacco above 52% of economic sales, pharma up 10 to 13%, courier growing high single digits; these legs are *working* even as transport drags.
- A fat, real dividend (a yield above roughly 6% implied by EUR 2.09 on EUR 33) backed by actual cash and a 12-year, roughly EUR 2bn-since-listing distribution record (a 236% total shareholder return since the 2014 listing).
- Profit-on-inventory has surprised to the upside three years running (EUR 25M to EUR 35M to EUR 45M) -- the un-guidable lever has been a tailwind, not a drag, so far.
- A latest-quarter turn (quarterly earnings per share up 84% year over year, trailing-twelve-month earnings per share of 3.02) -- a recent positive print that, if durable, undercuts the "terminal decline" read.

---

## 7. Hidden signals

- **Vocabulary migration:** "growth... diversification... new transactions" (2024) gives way to "optimize... compliance procedures... cost control... restructuring... turnaround" (2025). A textbook offense-to-defense shift in language inside 18 months.
- **The hedge that gives the game away:** the February 2024 decision to fix about 50% of the float at about 3.6% for three years is management quietly admitting the financial-income engine was about to roll -- they locked in *half* the tailwind before it faded. Read positively, it is prudent; read forensically, it dates exactly when management stopped believing rates would stay high.
- **Dividend framing creep:** "at least 90% of net profit" (full-year 2024) silently becomes "**despite the need to increase the payout ratio**" and a "99% payout" (full-year 2025). The policy headline is unchanged; the substance (coverage) deteriorated.
- **Acquisition posture reversal:** from "transformational... why not" (full-year 2024) to "more cautious... the fair answer is yes" (full-year 2025), explicitly because El Mosca burned them. The acquisition machine -- a core part of the bull story -- has been throttled by its own integration failure.
- **"One-off costs that will not roll over to '26"** (full-year 2025) -- management pre-committing that restructuring charges are confined to this year. This is a forward-looking promise to watch; if 2026 carries *more* El Mosca/Carbo charges, credibility takes a real hit.
- **Two finance and chief-executive voices, one consistent message:** Losada (chief financial officer) and Meiras/Amusco (chief executive officer) hand off cleanly; no internal dissonance in the narrative -- but also no fresh strategic voice introducing a new growth vector. The bench is steady but the playbook is unchanged.
- **Tobacco-tax dependency is now the de-facto earnings strategy:** three straight years of profit-on-inventory from tax-driven price hikes have become a structural assumption, even as management disclaims control. The business increasingly *needs* governments to keep raising tobacco taxes -- an odd, fragile dependency for a "defensive" name.

---

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Classification: pivoting (tilting toward deteriorating on the engines that matter).**

Logista is mid-pivot: a cash-rich, high-return tobacco-distribution franchise deliberately rotating into logistics, pharma, and courier to outlast the secular tobacco decline -- but the rotation has, so far, *imported* cyclical risk (El Mosca, Carbo) faster than it has built durable new earnings power, while the two tailwinds that flattered 2023 to 2024 (profit-on-inventory windfalls and a peak-rate cash float) are both fading or un-guidable. Earnings peaked in fiscal 2024 and rolled 9% lower in fiscal 2025; the dividend is now defended at a 99% payout rather than grown.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** *the durability of the dividend and of "normalized" earnings power once the rate-float income and profit-on-inventory windfalls normalize.* The entire deep-value case rests on whether EUR 33 is buying a stable roughly 6% yielder with a near-net-cash balance sheet and a slowly compounding diversified base (the cash-flow view, fair value of EUR 64) -- or a melting tobacco annuity whose surface cheapness is propped up by un-repeatable profit-on-inventory and a fading interest-rate tailwind, with a payout one bad year from a cut (the Buffett / earnings-power view, fair value of EUR 22.8, earnings-power margin of safety of -100%). There is no firm catalyst here -- no buyback authorization, no activist, no asset sale, no spin-off -- only a fat dividend and a cyclical-transport self-help turnaround. Size it as a yield-and-quality-value position with explicit profit-on-inventory and rate-sensitivity haircuts, not as an event-driven value re-rating.
